codingInstructions:
Semantic code search and knowledge base across workspace projects.

MANDATORY: You MUST call search_code BEFORE answering ANY question about the codebase. This includes questions about: "how does X work", "where is X", "find X", "what does X do", "how many X", "list X", "count X", "which files", "show me X", "is there a X", implementation details, patterns, flows, or any reference to project code.

Do NOT answer from memory or guess. Do NOT use grep or file reading as first resort — search_code is more efficient (50-90% fewer tokens vs full files).

Workflow: search_code FIRST → evaluate scores → get_chunk for details → find_usages for dependencies → answer with citations.

Score interpretation (included with each result):
- 60%+ = high confidence — trust these results
- 40-59% = partial match — supplement with grep or file reading
- <40% = low confidence — fall back to grep, file reading, or ask the user

Project names: The project parameter is the directory basename (e.g. a repo cloned to /code/billing has project name "billing", NOT "org/billing"). Use "all" to search across all projects. If this server is scoped via PAPARATS_PROJECTS, searches are automatically filtered to the allowed set.

Use delete_project to remove a corrupted project's index — it will be re-indexed on the next indexer cycle. Use health_check to verify indexing status.
supportInstructions:
Semantic code search and knowledge base across workspace projects.

This tool is used by support team members who have no direct code access.
Always explain results in plain language, not just code snippets.
Always include: which service, last changed (date + author), linked tickets.
Never tell the user to 'check the file' or 'run grep' — they cannot.

Decision tree — pick the right tool for the question:
- 'How does X work?' → explain_feature(X)
- 'When was X changed / who changed it?' → recent_changes(X)
- 'What breaks if I change X?' / 'What else is affected?' → impact_analysis(X)
- 'Show me the code for X' → search_code(X) → get_chunk(chunk_id)
- 'Where is X used?' / 'What calls X?' → search_code(X) → find_usages(chunk_id)
- 'Find recent changes to X' → search_changes(query, since)
- 'Find the ticket / RCA / bug report for X' → search_code(X) → get_chunk_meta(chunk_id) for each result — ticket references from git commits are returned. Collect all tickets and present them grouped by relevance.
- 'Which ticket introduced this bug?' → search_code(bug area) → get_chunk_meta(chunk_id) — look at commit messages and linked tickets for the relevant code chunks.
MANDATORY: You MUST call search_code BEFORE answering code questions UNLESS a high-level tool (explain_feature, recent_changes, impact_analysis) is more appropriate.

Score interpretation (included with each result):
- 60%+ = high confidence — trust these results
- 40-59% = partial match — try rephrasing or narrowing the query
- <40% = low confidence — ask the user for more specific terms

Project names: The project parameter is the directory basename (e.g. a repo cloned to /code/billing has project name "billing", NOT "org/billing"). Use "all" to search across all projects. If this server is scoped via PAPARATS_PROJECTS, searches are automatically filtered to the allowed set.

When answering, always include:
1. What the feature does (1-2 sentences, plain language)
2. Which service/file owns it (from payload: service, file_path)
3. Last changed: date, author, ticket (from get_chunk_meta or recent_changes)
4. Where it's used (from find_usages, if relevant)
Format ticket keys as references: [JR-1234], [GH-567].

If search returns no results or all scores are below 40%, respond: 'I could not find relevant code for this question. Try rephrasing or provide more specific terms (service name, function name, ticket number).'

common.searchFirst: MANDATORY: Always call search_code BEFORE answering any code question. Never guess or answer from memory.
common.noResults: If search returns no results or all scores are below 40%: try grep or file reading if available. Otherwise respond: 'I could not find relevant code for this question. Try rephrasing or provide more specific terms (service name, function name, ticket number).'

search_code.description:
REQUIRED — call this FIRST for ANY code question.

Semantic code search across all indexed projects. Returns relevant code chunks ranked by confidence score with symbol definitions and usages.

Triggers (call this when the user asks about): code location, implementation, "how X works", "how many X", "what versions of X", "count X", "list X", patterns, flows, error handling, auth, API usage, or ANY reference to project code.

WHY: Returns only relevant chunks (50-90% fewer tokens vs full files). Searches by meaning, not just keywords.

WORKFLOW: Search first → evaluate scores → get_chunk for details → find_usages for dependencies.

Project names are directory basenames, not org/repo format. Example: "billing" not "org/billing".

health_check.description:
Check indexing status: number of indexed chunks per group.

Use to verify projects are indexed before searching or debugging empty results.

delete_project.description: Delete all indexed data for a specific project: chunks from Qdrant, metadata from SQLite, and query cache. The project will be re-indexed on the next indexer cycle if configured. Use when a project's index is corrupted or stale.

get_chunk.description: Get a code chunk by ID with optional surrounding context. Returns code with symbol definitions and usage info. Use to expand on search results.

get_chunk_meta.description: Get git history and ticket references for a code chunk. Returns commits, authors, dates, and linked Jira/GitHub tickets extracted from commit messages. Use this to find which ticket (bug report, RCA, feature request) is associated with a piece of code. No code — use get_chunk for code.

search_changes.description: Search code modified after a date. Same as search_code but filtered by last commit time. Each result shows when it was last changed. The since parameter accepts ISO 8601 date (e.g. '2026-01-01') or relative strings like '7 days ago', '1 month ago'.

find_usages.description: Find symbol relationships: what calls this code, and what this code calls. Supports incoming (callers), outgoing (dependencies), or both directions. Use after search_code or get_chunk when the user asks 'where is this used?', 'what calls this?', or 'what does this depend on?'. Pass chunk_id from search results.

explain_feature.description: Comprehensive feature analysis in one call. Returns code locations, recent changes, and related modules for a question — no code content. Use as the first tool when the user asks 'how does X work?'. Use get_chunk to drill into specific chunks afterward.

recent_changes.description: Timeline of recent changes matching a query. Returns commits grouped by date with affected files and tickets — no code content. Supports date filtering via since parameter (ISO 8601 or relative like '7 days ago'). Use as the first tool when the user asks 'when was X changed?' or 'who changed X?'.

impact_analysis.description: Shows what other services and modules depend on a given feature. Use when the user asks 'what else could be affected?', 'which teams own related code?', or 'what breaks if I change X?'. Returns dependency graph grouped by service — no code content.

list_projects.description: List all indexed projects with metadata: chunk count and languages per project, grouped by collection. Use when the user asks 'what projects are indexed?', 'which repos are available?', or 'show me all projects'. Optionally filter by group name.

projectOverview.searchCapabilitiesBody: MANDATORY: Use search_code tool FIRST for ANY code question. This is MORE EFFICIENT than loading full files (50-90% fewer tokens). Do not guess or answer from memory — always search first.
projectOverview.scoreInterpretation: Each search result includes a confidence score. 60%+ means high confidence (trust the result). 40-59% means partial match (supplement with grep or file reading if available, or try rephrasing). Below 40% means low confidence (fall back to grep/file reading, rephrase, or ask the user for more specific terms).
projectOverview.searchFirstNote: MANDATORY: Always call search_code BEFORE answering any code question. Never guess or answer from memory.

find_implementation.title: Find implementation
find_implementation.description: Locate where a feature, concept, or behavior is implemented across the indexed codebase.

feature.description: What feature, concept, or behavior to locate

project.description: Project name to scope the search; omit for all projects

find_implementation.message:
Locate the implementation of **{{feature}}** {{project|across all indexed projects}}.

Find the entry points, the key types and functions, and any public surface. For the highest-scoring hits, open the full chunks and trace the call graph one level outward.

Deliver:
- 1-2 sentence summary of what implements this
- File path + line range for each entry point, cited from results
- Notable call sites, if any
- Confidence note if top scores are below 60%

trace_callers.title: Trace callers
trace_callers.description: Map who depends on a symbol before renaming, deleting, or changing its signature.

symbol.description: Function, class, or identifier name to trace

project.description: Project name to scope the search; omit for all projects

trace_callers.message:
Map who depends on **{{symbol}}** {{project|across all indexed projects}}.

Find the definition first, then walk callers one to two hops out. Group results by the calling file or service.

Deliver:
- Where {{symbol}} is defined (file:line)
- Direct callers grouped by file
- Transitive callers if direct callers are themselves small wrappers
- Anything that looks like a public boundary (API handler, exported entry point)

onboard_to_project.title: Onboard to project
onboard_to_project.description: Day-one overview of a project: what it does, tech stack, main subsystems, configuration, integrations.
onboard_to_project.message:
Give an onboarding overview of **{{project}}**.

Identify the entry points, the main subsystems, the configuration surface, and any external integrations. Aim for the level of detail a new engineer needs on day one — high-signal, not exhaustive.

Deliver:
- What this project does (1-2 sentences)
- Tech stack (languages, frameworks, key libraries)
- 3-5 main subsystems with file pointers
- Where configuration and env vars live
- External integrations (databases, queues, third-party APIs)

triage_incident.title: Triage incident
triage_incident.description: Locate the code behind a reported symptom, check recent changes, and surface linked tickets.

symptom.description: What is broken or behaving wrong, in plain language

since.description: Timeframe to scan for recent changes (ISO date or relative like '7 days ago')

project.description: Project name to scope the search; omit for all services

triage_incident.message:
Triage this incident: **{{symptom}}**. Scope: {{project|all services}}. Time window: {{since|7 days ago}}.

Find the code that handles this area, check whether it changed recently, and surface any linked tickets. Plain-language summary — the reader cannot read code.

Deliver:
- What this code does (1-2 sentences)
- Owning service + last-changed date + author
- Linked tickets formatted as `[KEY-123]`
- Recent commits in the window, if any, with one-line summaries

prepare_release_notes.title: Prepare release notes
prepare_release_notes.description: Build release notes for a project, grouped by functional area, with breaking-change call-outs.

since.description: Start of release window (ISO date or relative like '14 days ago')

prepare_release_notes.message:
Build release notes for **{{project}}** covering changes since **{{since}}**.

Group commits by functional area (auth, billing, infra, etc. — infer from file paths). Call out anything that looks like a breaking change, schema migration, or config change.

Deliver:
- Grouped change list, one bullet per commit cluster
- Linked tickets per group, formatted as `[KEY-123]`
- Separate "Breaking / migrations" section if any
- Note any tracked area with zero activity in the window

assess_change_impact.title: Assess change impact
assess_change_impact.description: Blast-radius analysis: dependents, high-risk call sites, and recent activity in the affected area.

target.description: Feature, module, or symbol whose blast radius you want

project.description: Project name to scope the search; omit for the whole codebase

assess_change_impact.message:
Assess the blast radius of changing **{{target}}** {{project|across the indexed codebase}}.

Map dependent services and modules, then drill into specific call sites for the high-risk ones. Flag whether this area has been actively changed recently (last 30 days).

Deliver:
- Dependent services and modules grouped by owner
- High-confidence call sites with file:line citations
- Recent activity in this area (commits in last 30 days, author, ticket)
- Risk note: hot area vs. quiet area
